title: Basketry weave types: a guide
subtitle: The five main willow weave patterns and when to use each one
excerpt: Willow basketry uses five core weave types: randing, pairing, waling, chase-weave, and fitching. Each has a specific structural purpose. This reading explains what each weave looks like, what it does, and which part of a basket it belongs in.
sourceNotes: Cooke The Basketmaker (1882) for the weave nomenclature and structural descriptions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Single-rod weave: one rod passes in front of one stake and behind the next. The simplest and fastest side weave.
Two-rod weave where two weavers alternate with each other in front of one stake and behind the next. Produces a tighter and more even surface than randing.
Three-rod weave where each rod passes in front of two stakes and behind one, in rotation. Used for structural rows at the upsett, mid-side, and top edge.
Two weavers worked simultaneously in opposite directions, one chasing the other. Produces a balanced even surface and uses material efficiently.
A twisted two-rod weave used for open decorative areas, such as the lower sides of log baskets where air circulation is needed.

content.text: Every willow basket is built from a small set of weave patterns combined in a specific order. Choosing the wrong weave for a section does not always cause failure, but it usually wastes material or produces a weaker or less attractive result. The five patterns below cover almost every basket made in the British tradition.
Randing
The simplest weave: one rod, in front of one stake and behind the next, all the way round.
Randing

is used for the open body of light baskets such as herb trugs and egg baskets. Because a single rod spirals continuously, a basket with an odd number of stakes will self-correct the over-under pattern; a basket with an even number of stakes requires two weavers started at opposite points. Randing on an even-stake basket with one weaver produces a flat band rather than a continuous spiral.
Pairing
Two rods used together, twisting around each other as they pass each stake.
Pairing

is used for the base weave (where it ties the base spokes evenly and keeps them from spreading unevenly) and for the tie-in rows after bye-stake insertion. Pairing produces a denser, more balanced surface than randing and will work on either an odd or even stake count.
Waling
Three rods worked in rotation, each passing in front of two stakes and behind one.
Waling

is the structural weave: used for the upsett rows (which pull stakes vertical and set the basket profile), for mid-side strengthening rows on deep baskets, and for the top edge before the border. Four rows of waling at the upsett, worked in both directions to balance the twist, is standard for most baskets. Waling done only in one direction will cause the basket to twist slightly as it dries.
Chase-weave

Two weavers working simultaneously, one directly behind the other, spiralling in the same direction. Each weaver chases the other round; when the lead weaver completes a row, the following weaver is one row behind it.
Chase-weave
produces a finely packed surface that uses shorter rods efficiently. It is favoured for shopping baskets where a dense, strong side wall is needed and the rods available are short.
Fitching
Two rods twisted around each other between each stake, creating a visible open twist pattern.
Fitching
is used decoratively and practically on log baskets and storage baskets where open sides are an advantage. It requires more material than randing but the open spiral is visually distinctive.
Choosing the right weave
Base: always pairing. The two-rod twist spaces the base spokes evenly as they open out from the slath.
Upsett and top edge: always waling. The three-rod rotation provides the structural rigidity these zones need.
Light open sides: randing on an odd stake count, or chase-weave on an even stake count.
Dense heavy-duty sides: chase-weave. Uses shorter rods and packs tighter than randing.
Decorative open zones: fitching, typically on the lower half of a log or storage basket.
